content.problemStatement:
An array is called good if it has exactly 222 distinct elements. For example, [1,1,2,1,2],[5,3][1, 1, 2, 1, 2], [5, 3][1,1,2,1,2],[5,3] are good but [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3] and [1,1,1][1, 1, 1][1,1,1] are not.

You are given an array AAA of NNN elements and you should find the length of the **shortest** possible good subarray†^{\dagger}† of AAA. If no good subarray exists, print −1-1−1 instead.

†^{\dagger}† BBB is said to be an subarray of AAA if BBB can be formed by deleting a (possibly empty) prefix of AAA and a (possibly empty) suffix of AAA. For example, [4,1,3],[3,5][4, 1, 3], [3, 5][4,1,3],[3,5] and [4,1,3,5,2][4, 1, 3, 5, 2][4,1,3,5,2] are subarrays of [4,1,3,5,2][4, 1, 3, 5, 2][4,1,3,5,2] but [5,3][5, 3][5,3] and [1,5][1, 5][1,5] are not.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each line contains NNN - the length of the array.
The second line contains NNN integers - A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the length of the shortest good subarray of AAA, or −1-1−1 if none exist.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : [5,3][5, 3][5,3] itself is good, and is the shortest possible good subarray of AAA.
Test Case 2 : There are no good subarrays because the only subarray contains 111 distinct element only.
